title: Needle felting over a wire armature
subtitle: Building poseable figures by wrapping and needling wool around a wire skeleton
excerpt: A wire armature provides the skeleton for needle-felted figures that would be too thin or too tall to stand unsupported in solid felt alone. The wire is wrapped in wool roving, then needle-felted to compact the wool into a firm coating. Armature figures can be repositioned into different poses and hold their shape indefinitely.
sourceNotes: Wire armature needle felting is an established technique in contemporary figure-making. Observation-led.

glossaryTerms.definition: A wire skeleton used as the internal structure of a needle-felted figure. The armature holds the pose and provides a core around which wool is built up layer by layer.

The technique of pulling thin strips of roving and winding them tightly around the wire armature before needling begins. Roving wrapping fills out the form and holds the roving in place for initial needling.

content.text: allows shapes that solid felt cannot hold: a figure standing on thin legs, a branch with fine extensions, a bird with outspread wings. The wire provides the structural core; the wool builds up the surface. A
begins the process before the needle is used.

attrs.title: Needle safety
attrs.body: Never needle toward fingers holding the armature wire. Hold the wire ends, not the middle. Foam pad required: the needle must not contact metal.

items.name: Armature wire, 1 mm aluminium or copper

content.text: For a simple human-proportioned figure: cut two equal lengths of wire, each long enough to form the full body plus one limb pair. Fold each length in half. Twist the two folded wires together at the centre for 2 to 3 cm to form the torso. The four free ends become two arms and two legs. Bend the feet upward so the figure stands flat. Wrap a small piece of masking tape around any sharp wire end.

Pull the roving into a thin strip about 5 mm wide. Starting from the feet, wind the strip tightly around the wire legs, working upward. Overlap each wrap to avoid gaps. Continue up through the torso and along the arms. The wire should be fully covered and the figure should have a rough body shape at this stage.

Place the figure on the foam pad. Needle the limbs with the medium needle, rotating the figure as you work to felt all sides. Use short strokes (1 to 2 cm depth) and stab perpendicular to the wire rather than parallel to it. The wool will compact around the wire.

Add small tufts of roving to the torso, upper arms, and thighs to build up mass. Needle each tuft in place. The head is usually felted separately (a solid ball on a short wire neck) and pushed over the top wire end.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Two common problems with wire armature figures.

items.symptom: Wool keeps sliding along the wire rather than staying in place
items.cause: Wire surface is too smooth for the roving to grip.
items.fix: Wrap the wire first with a layer of masking tape before applying roving. The tape surface grips the fibres.

items.symptom: Needle keeps bending or breaking on the wire
items.cause: Needle is contacting the wire itself, not the wool over the wire.
items.fix: Build up a thicker roving wrap before needling. The needle should never contact bare metal; there should always be at least 5 mm of wool between needle tip and wire.
